name: Count Basie
abstract: William James "Count" Basie (; August 21, 1904 – April 26, 1984) was an American jazz pianist, organist, bandleader, and composer. In 1935, he formed the Count Basie Orchestra, and in 1936 took them to Chicago for a long engagement and their first recording.

works_created.t: Sent For You Yesterday (Here You Come Today)

quotes.text: Yes, I remember it very well. It was a great concert, and that was my first time at Carnegie Hall onstage. John had once taken me to hear Marion Anderson, which is something I will never forget.
quotes.caption: Count Basie recalls his first Carnegie Hall performance in 1938

quotes.text: Yeah, I know that was the first time I had ever been on the stage at Carnegie Hall.
quotes.caption: Recalling his first Carnegie Hall performance in 1938

quotes.text: Oh, you've heard it. But that was a great time, the concert with Bennie, and the thrill of just being and working at Carnegie Hall -- it sort of gave you goose pimples, you know. (Inaudible) My fingers were kinda damp when I started to play.
quotes.caption: Basie recalls playing with Benny Goodman at Carnegie Hall

about_quotes.speaker: Walter Booker and Jimmy Cobb
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee narrates historical facts about Count Basie's band and Joe Williams, stating 'when he was with Count Basie’s band' and 'I remember seeing Duke Ellington’s band and the Count Basie band', but does not describe a personal interaction or relationship with Count Basie himself.
about_quotes.doc_title: Walter Booker and Jimmy Cobb interviewed by Michael Woods, Majesty of the Seas, May 30, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Lou Marini
about_quotes.evidence: Frank Wess told me that there were nights on the Basie band when everybody breathed together. He told me that they went to London one time and Basie said I don't know why I’m paying all this money to ship the parts to London, you guys never look at the book anyway.
about_quotes.doc_title: Lou Marini interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, June 15, 2023

about_quotes.speaker: Al Grey
about_quotes.evidence: I spent almost 20 years of my life off and on with Basie... I joined the band... Count Basie called me back to his dressing room and said, 'well we’re getting ready to go to England in the next five days, and we’d like to take you along with us'
about_quotes.doc_title: Al Grey interviewed by Michael Woods, Scottsdale, Arizona, March 3, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Eddie Metz Jr.
about_quotes.evidence: He said, 'we want you to join the band starting October 8.' ... I was 24 years old and no rehearsal. My rehearsal was in the dressing room with Basie saying, 'Is there anything I should know here? I’m only 24 years old why did I get this gig.'
about_quotes.doc_title: Eddie Metz Jr. interviewed by Monk Rowe, New Hartford, New York, October 3, 2015

about_quotes.speaker: Tom McIntosh
about_quotes.evidence: I knew about Count Basie, Duke Ellington and all the major jazz guys... They all played in a major theatre in Baltimore that was the talk of the town. But, every week someone would be killed in that area so my father would never let me go.
about_quotes.doc_title: Tom McIntosh — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: John Heard
about_quotes.evidence: I got arrested, and the judge told me, “You spend three years with Basie or I’m sending me to the military.” ... ‘73 to about ‘75 or ‘76, somewhere around there. ... Then I went from Ahmad Jamal to Count Basie.
about_quotes.doc_title: John Heard interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, Scottsdale, Arizona, March 4, 1995
